title: A startup claims it broke through a bottleneck that’s holding back LLMs
gist: Miami startup Subquadratic claims its SubQ model replaces the transformer's quadratic dense-attention with a dynamic sparse-attention mechanism, delivering frontier-level coding performance at a fraction of the cost and 56x the speed, validated by third-party tests from Appen—though skeptics note it bootstrapped from Qwen weights and remains largely unavailable.

insights.claim: Subquadratic's SubQ model ditches the transformer's dense attention in favor of a dynamic sparse-attention mechanism that selects only some token relationships to compute, aiming to break the quadratic-compute bottleneck that has constrained LLMs since 2017.
insights.speaker: Will Douglas Heaven
insights.so_what: If validated, a sub-quadratic architecture would attack the single largest cost and energy driver in LLM inference—watch for incumbents and chip demand exposure.
insights.quote: Subquadratic's solution is to ditch dense attention, the core operation of a transformer, in favor of what's known as sparse attention, which slashes the number of computations needed.

insights.claim: Subquadratic's differentiation is that its sparse-attention mechanism dynamically selects which token relationships matter on the fly, differing per input, rather than using the fixed patterns prior sparse-attention attempts relied on.
insights.speaker: Will Douglas Heaven
insights.so_what: Dynamic selection is the technical moat claim—if it holds, fixed-pattern sparse methods become obsolete; the firm keeps the selection method secret.
insights.quote: one of the things that makes our mechanism unique is that we dynamically select which ones are important.

insights.claim: Subquadratic's CEO predicts the transformer architecture itself will be displaced, stating nobody will be building on transformers within a few years.
insights.speaker: Will Douglas Heaven
insights.so_what: A direct bet against the dominant LLM architecture—an extreme claim that would reorder the entire model-building stack if even partly true.
insights.quote: We don't think anybody will be building on transformers in a few years.

insights.claim: Independent tester Appen found SubQ ran 56 times faster than models using FlashAttention, a prior sparse-attention technique, in a raw speed test.
insights.speaker: Will Douglas Heaven
insights.so_what: A 56x theoretical speed edge, if it translates to real workloads, is a step-change in inference economics for latency-sensitive tasks.
insights.quote: Appen found that SubQ was 56 times faster than models using FlashAttention, a previous sparse-attention technique.

insights.claim: Subquadratic claims running Anthropic's Opus 4.6 through Nvidia's RULER 128 retrieval test cost $2,600, versus $8 for SubQ—a roughly 325x cost difference on that benchmark.
insights.speaker: Justin Dangel
insights.so_what: A two-orders-of-magnitude cost claim on long-context retrieval; unverifiable until SubQ is broadly available but directly threatens incumbent per-token economics.

insights.claim: SubQ has a context window of up to 12 million tokens—roughly 12x the one-million-token windows of most top models today—and scored 98% on Needle-in-a-Haystack retrieval at six- and 12-million-token scales per Appen.
insights.speaker: Will Douglas Heaven
insights.so_what: A 12M-token window with near-perfect retrieval would unlock whole-codebase and multi-hundred-document tasks that current models can't load.
insights.quote: sustaining near-perfect long-context retrieval at scales few models are tested at.

insights.claim: SubQ scored 89.7% on LiveCodeBench, placing it in the same range as other top coding models, per Appen.
insights.speaker: Will Douglas Heaven
insights.so_what: Frontier-level coding scores at far lower cost would make SubQ a credible challenger specifically in the coding-assistant segment.
insights.quote: This model continues to provide frontier-level performance in coding,

insights.claim: Subquadratic bootstrapped SubQ by reusing weights from the Chinese open-source Qwen model rather than training from scratch, which undercuts its claim to have fully reinvented how LLMs work.
insights.speaker: Will Douglas Heaven
insights.so_what: The Qwen-weight reuse is the strongest counter to the 'reinvented LLMs' narrative—suggests an architectural retrofit, not a ground-up breakthrough.
insights.quote: it cuts across Subquadratic's claim that it has fully reinvented how LLMs work.

insights.claim: A former OpenAI researcher cautions that while Subquadratic may have built something real, the public evidence does not yet justify its stronger claim of having solved the quadratic-attention bottleneck.
insights.speaker: Will Douglas Heaven
insights.so_what: Expert skepticism plus the model's limited availability means the claim should be treated as unproven; verification gates any investment thesis.
insights.quote: the public evidence does not yet justify the stronger claim that they have solved the quadratic attention bottleneck.

insights.claim: Subquadratic says tens of thousands of users and more than 500 enterprise customers have signed up for early access, but the firm has given very few people access so far, limiting independent verification.
insights.speaker: Will Douglas Heaven
insights.so_what: Strong demand signal but a closed-access posture; broad availability is the key catalyst to watch for de-risking the claims.
